Unity can be activated by certain cues of kinship, even when no familiar relationships exist. For example, the closer you have lived to someone, the more likely you are to assume the person is genetically similar to you. This is an understandable assumption given that, for most of our history, humans lived in small groups of genetically similar individuals, in their tribes, clans, and bands. Because that assumption has almost always been true for us as a species, it became lodged in our expectations, where it persists to this day. You are likely to feel more self-other overlap with, and consequently more favoritism toward, those who grew up near you or live in your community, even for others you don't know personally. This greater favoritism is known as localism, so whenever you uncover information that someone you'd like to influence lives or has lived in the same place as you, be sure to remark on it to that individual.
